title: The Best Super Bowl Trailers and Movies in the ’90s, According to Chuck Klosterman
format: Sean Fennessey and Joanna Robinson run down the Super Bowl trailer glut and Kenneth Branagh's new Poirot, then Chuck Klosterman visits to talk about the movies in his book The Nineties.

films.title: Animal House
films.note: Reitman produced it, one of several Canadian productions he backed before his own directing run.

films.title: Kindergarten Cop
films.note: Sean's pick for a favorite Reitman movie.

films.title: Dave
films.note: Joanna's pick — she, Bill Simmons, and Amanda Dobbins did a Rewatchables episode on it in 2019.

films.title: Up in the Air
films.note: Ivan produced; he and son Jason earned a shared Oscar nomination for it.

films.title: Ghostbusters: Afterlife
films.note: Their last collaboration before Ivan's death at 75.

films.title: Nope
films.note: Jordan Peele's third feature. Sean drafted it in the movie auction and calls the trailer a masterclass in giving away nothing but vibe.

films.title: Deep Water
films.note: Adrian Lyne's long-delayed Ben Affleck/Ana de Armas erotic thriller, teased via a Hulu drop rather than a trailer proper.

films.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
films.note: Sam Raimi leaning into horror; Joanna clocks what sounds like Patrick Stewart's voice and a rumored Tom Cruise variant cameo.

films.title: Doctor Strange
films.note: Joanna's least favorite Marvel movie, brought up only by comparison.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The cameo-chasing high the sequel is rumored to be reaching for.

films.title: Jurassic World Dominion
films.note: Colin Trevorrow's back in the chair; both hosts have made peace with this being the last one.

films.title: Jurassic Park
films.note: Both call it a perfect film that nothing since has touched.

films.title: The Lost World: Jurassic Park
films.note: Sean says it has real problems but still likes parts of it.

films.title: Jurassic World
films.note: Sean had fun with it, just not Jurassic Park-elegant fun.

films.title: The Batman
films.note: Three weeks out at the time of taping.

films.title: Aquaman
films.note: Both call it a perfect piece of junk-food cinema — the first time DC got the tone right, in their view.

films.title: Wonder Woman
films.note: The other DC movie that made them think the studio had figured it out.

films.title: Death on the Nile
films.note: Branagh's second Poirot outing. Both find it visually plasticine and are baffled by the accent choices, but enjoy the mustache-origin cold open as unintentional comedy.

films.title: Death on the Nile (1978)
films.note: Joanna's benchmark — Peter Ustinov's Poirot and a stacked cast (Mia Farrow, Bette Davis, Maggie Smith, Angela Lansbury) shot on real locations.

films.title: Murder on the Orient Express
films.note: Branagh's first Poirot film; set expectations lower than the 1978 Death on the Nile did.

films.title: Exodus: Gods and Kings
films.note: Joanna's comparison point for the same over-lit, location-shot-but-fake look.

films.title: Much Ado About Nothing
films.note: Joanna calls it one of the greatest films ever made, part of her general affection for Branagh.

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Referenced as Branagh's big awards-season moment this year.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Cited alongside his Poirot films as evidence Branagh is now happily chewing scenery.

films.title: Titanic
films.note: The hinge point Klosterman says movie culture pivoted on.

films.title: Reality Bites
films.note: His entry point for the decade's idea of selling out — a film about that very subject that is itself a sellout product.

films.title: Falling Down
films.note: The Michael Douglas rampage film Klosterman says the culture has chosen to half-forget because its ideas are too uncomfortable now.

films.title: The Matrix
films.note: His pick for the single most significant film of the decade — a movie, he argues, about the internet before most people had it.

films.title: Kids
films.note: Used in the book to illustrate how much language has shifted since.

films.title: In the Company of Men
films.note: Paired with Kids for the same point about language.

films.title: The Blair Witch Project
films.note: His example of a marketing con — total information vacuum — that couldn't be pulled off today.

films.title: Reservoir Dogs
films.note: His stand-in for the walk-in-blind theater experience the internet has erased.

films.title: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button
films.note: Klosterman recalls loving it at an early press screening with no context, then remembering it as bad years later.

films.title: Meet Joe Black
films.note: His example of the most financially successful movie ever among films nobody actually watched — audiences bought tickets just to see the Phantom Menace trailer attached.

films.title: The Sixth Sense
films.note: Reviewed at the time for the Akron Beacon Journal; he argues it's well-made independent of its twist.

films.title: Fight Club
films.note: He'd read the novel first, so the twist didn't land the same way it did for other critics.

films.title: The Village
films.note: His example of a twist-reliant film where he'd guessed the reveal twenty minutes in.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: The last thing he'd seen — he liked it, ranks it middle of the pack for Paul Thomas Anderson.

films.title: Punch-Drunk Love
films.note: The one PTA film he doesn't like, over its sympathy for a character who can't control his anger.

films.title: Saturday Night Fever
films.note: His example of a movie that became a period piece by accident, not by design.

films.title: Slacker
films.note: Seeing it on video in 1992 is the one movie experience he says actually changed his life.

TV: Lord of the Rings: The Rings of Power

Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madn.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness

The Curious Case of Benjamin Button.title: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button
